title: Chapter Sixteen: The Game, Part II
content: I have read Jessel's journal three times.

The first time, I read it the way you read a diagnosis — skimming for the lethal words, the sentence that reorganizes everything you thought you understood about your own body. The second time, I read it methodically, tracking the structure beneath the horror. The third time, I read it the way you read a will, because that's what it is. Jessel's last testament. A map in ink faded to rust, her handwriting deteriorating as the rounds progressed, the letters of a woman who stopped sleeping on schedule and started sleeping with one eye open.
The Game.
That's what Peter called it. That's what it has always been.

Two players on one side — the twins — and one player on the other: the governess. Seven rounds, seven weeks, each round a calibrated escalation designed to test a specific threshold of human endurance. If the governess survives all seven without breaking — without fleeing, without surrendering to madness, without checking herself into a hospital and recounting the whole thing to a psychiatrist who will see the architecture of a breakdown rather than the architecture of a trap — she wins. The prize: a trust fund Peter established before he died, accessible only upon certified completion. The money returns to the estate if the governess breaks. The house always wins, unless the governess doesn't fold.
It's a game. It was always a game.
And Peter Quint — my father — was its architect.

The leather of the journal is soft from handling, the binding cracked where I've pressed it flat. I can smell the old paper, something between dust and dried flowers, the faint chemical edge of the ink beneath. My room is cold. The radiator clicks and says nothing. Outside, the Bly grounds are grey with early morning fog, the lake erased, the trees smeared like charcoal.
I don't plan to run.

Round 1: Displaced objects. Small at first. A brush moved from the vanity to the bedstand. A book closed that you left open. Shoes rearranged by the door. The aim is disorientation — the governess begins to doubt her own memory, her own routines. She starts checking. Double-checking. The first hairline fracture in the wall of certainty.

Round 2: Ghost stories. The twins begin to narrate. They see figures at windows. They hear voices in empty rooms. They produce "evidence" — photographs with lens flares they call spirits, audio recordings of feedback they call whispers. The governess's logical objections are treated with patient, childish condescension. The aim is to introduce the supernatural as a framework. Once the governess accepts that these things *could* be ghosts, she's already lost the ground she stood on.

Round 3: Sleep disruption. Alarms at 3 a.m. A record player turning itself on. The governess's door opening in the night when no one is there — or when someone is, standing in the hall, gone by the time she finds the light. The aim is exhaustion. A sleep-deprived mind is a credulous mind. A credulous mind is a breakable mind.

Round 4: Chemical interference. Jessel's handwriting tightens here. Something in the tea. Something in the water. Mild sedatives, then something stronger — enough to make her foggy, enough to make her doubt her own perceptions. The aim is to erode the boundary between what she experienced and what she might have imagined.

Round 5: Social isolation. Letters that don't arrive. Phone calls that don't connect. Visitors turned away at the gate with messages the governess never sent. The world shrinks to Bly and its inhabitants. The aim is to remove outside reference — to make the house the only reality, the twins the only witnesses, the Game the only narrative.

Round 6: Medicalization. The twins express concern. They tell Mrs. Grose the governess is unwell. They describe episodes the governess doesn't remember — because of Round 4. They produce a paper trail. A doctor is called. The governess is told she is ill. She is told she needs rest. She is told she needs help. The aim is to shift the frame: the governess is no longer a reliable narrator of her own experience. She is a patient. Patients don't get believed.
Round 7: Violence.

Jessel's entry for Round 7 is half a page. She never experienced it. She broke through the wall at Round 6 — not broke down, broke *through* — and discovered the trust, discovered the rules, discovered the architecture of the thing that had been happening to her for six weeks. She wasn't mad. She wasn't imagining it. She was being *played*.
So they escalated. Out of sequence. Out of design.

Jessel wrote: *They came for me in the night. Not the children. Men from the village. Orderlies. They had papers. They had a signature — whose signature, I don't know. They called it a hold. They called it care. I bit one of them and ran. I made it to the gate before they caught me.*
And then: *Round 7 is when they stop performing and start hurting. But Peter's daughter will have something I didn't. She'll have knowledge from the start. She can choose to play backwards.*
I close the journal. My fingers are stiff. The radiator clicks again, a sound like a bone setting.

Three weeks at Bly. Three weeks, and I've survived Rounds 1 through 6 already. Displaced objects — I noticed, I catalogued, I told myself I was forgetful. Ghost stories — Flora's visitors, Miles's descriptions, the figure at the lake. Sleep disruption — the waking at 3 a.m., the exhaustion I attributed to the damp. Chemical interference — the tea. God, the tea. Social isolation — the letters from No One, the internet my only thread, cut when the world crawled into my inbox with my own hospital records. Medicalization — Mrs. Grose's concern, the doctor's visit, the paper they wanted me to sign.
I've been playing the Game since I arrived. I just didn't know I was playing.
Now I do.

The breakfast room smells of toast and marmalade and something floral — Flora's shampoo, or the roses Mrs. Grose puts in the vase by the window. The light is thin, the fog still pressing against the glass. I can taste the bitter dregs of the tea I didn't drink, cold and untouched at the centre of the table. I haven't eaten. My body is running on something other than food.

Miles is reading. Flora is arranging the marmalade into a line along the edge of her plate, a precise geometry I now recognize as something she does when she's performing the act of a child while the adult part of her works.
I set Jessel's journal on the table.

The leather makes a sound — not a slap, not a thud, something between, the sound of evidence hitting wood. The journal lands between the toast rack and the butter dish. A dead woman's voice sitting next to the marmalade.
Miles looks at the journal. Then at me. Then at the journal again.
"I know about the Game."
I say it plainly. No tremor. No accusation. Just a fact laid on the table like the book itself.
Silence.
The fog presses against the window. The radiator clicks. Flora's hand stops moving on the marmalade.

Miles picks up his teacup. Takes a sip. His throat moves. He sets the cup down on its saucer with a small, precise sound — ceramic on ceramic, the kind of sound that belongs in a drawing room, not a confessional.
"Of course you do." He dabs his mouth with a napkin. "You're Peter's daughter. He'd have told you."
"He didn't tell me anything. He died when I was four."
Miles smiles. Not a grin — the kind of smile that arrives when a problem resolves itself elegantly, when a piece fits into a space you were worried would stay empty.
"Exactly."

I feel the word land. *Exactly.* As if my ignorance was the point. As if Peter designed the Game to be inherited by someone who would come in blind — who would experience every round as reality, who would break or survive on her own nature and not her foreknowledge. A fair test requires a naive subject.
But Jessel changed that. Jessel left the journal. And I found it.
"The seven rounds," I say. "I've survived six."

"Have you?" Miles leans back in his chair. He's wearing a grey jumper, sleeves pushed to his elbows. His forearms are lean, the tendons visible — the hands of someone who does precise, patient things in the night without being heard. "Which six?"
"Displaced objects. Ghost stories. Sleep disruption. Chemical interference. Social isolation. Medicalization."
I list them like a receipt. Like items I've already paid for.
Miles nods slowly, as if confirming a sum. "And Round 7?"
"Violence."
"Violence," he repeats. He likes the word. I can hear him tasting it. "Yes. That's the one you should be worried about."
"Should I?"
"That depends." He folds his napkin. Sets it beside his plate. The gesture is precise, final, like a card laid face-up. "Are you going to run?"
"No."
"Are you going to break?"
"No."
"Then you should be worried." He smiles again. "But not too worried. Worried enough to be interesting."

Flora hasn't spoken. She's watching me with those eyes — the pale, unreadable eyes that have always seemed older than her face. She's sitting very still. The marmalade is in its line, a row of amber soldiers. Her fingers are linked around her juice glass, and she doesn't blink, and I realize with a small, cold clarity that Flora is the one I should be watching. Miles is performing. Miles is enjoying himself. But Flora is *calculating*.
"The trust fund," I say. "I want it."
"Of course you do." Miles waves a hand. "It's yours if you win. Peter's design. The terms are clear."
"Then we continue."

"We continue," he agrees. "But not as before. You've shown your hand, Maeve. You've read the journal. You know the structure. The old Game — the secret one, the one where we pretended and you doubted — that's over. We need new rules."
I wait. Somewhere in the house, Mrs. Grose is moving — I can hear her footsteps in the corridor above, the particular creak of the fourth step she always avoids.
"No more secrecy," Miles says. He says it like a proposal. Like an investment pitch. "No more pretense. We'll tell you what round we're in. You'll know what's coming. A fair game."
Fair. The word sits on the table between us like the journal, like the cold tea, like the butter knife I'm suddenly aware of — its edge, its weight, its proximity to Miles's hand.
"And in exchange?"

"In exchange, you don't leave." He says it gently, but the gentleness is a technique. I recognize it from Round 5. "You play to the end. You see it through. If you run, the money reverts. The Game resets. A new governess. Another seven weeks. And your father's work continues without you."

My father's work. The phrase turns in my chest like a key. Miles speaks about it with the reverence of a disciple, the possessiveness of an heir. The Game is his inheritance — his religion. And he's been waiting for a worthy opponent, someone who knows the rules, who won't crumble in Round 4, who won't dissolve into doubt.
He's been waiting for Peter's daughter to stand up and play.
I'm standing up.
"Agreed," I say.
Miles extends his hand.

His hand is cold. I feel it the instant our palms meet — the dry, deliberate cold of someone whose circulation runs on something other than blood. His grip is firm. Mine is firmer. We shake once, the way deals are sealed in rooms that don't have fog pressing against the windows.

Flora watches. She hasn't moved. The marmalade line is perfect. Her eyes travel from our joined hands to my face, and I can't read her expression but I can feel its weight, the way you feel a stare on the back of your neck in a room you thought was empty.
"Round 7 begins tomorrow," Miles says. He releases my hand. "I'll give you today to prepare."
"How generous."
"I'm not generous." He picks up his teacup again. "I'm competitive. A weak opponent isn't satisfying. I want you at full strength. I want to earn it."

I want to smash the cup out of his hand. I want to grab the butter knife — I want to do something loud and physical that shatters the civilised architecture of this breakfast, this negotiation, this handshake. But I don't. I stand. I pick up Jessel's journal from the table. The leather is warm now from the room, from the table, from the proximity of toast and tea and the performative domesticity of two children who were never children.
"One more thing," I say.
Miles raises an eyebrow.
"Jessel's journal describes seven rounds. She only made it to six. What happens after Round 7?"

Miles sips his tea. Over the rim of the cup, his eyes meet mine, and I see it — a flicker, quick and controlled, of something he didn't expect me to ask. Not alarm. Not fear. *Interest.* The interest of a player whose opponent has just made a move they didn't predict.
"Peter designed seven rounds," he says. "Seven is the structure."
"But you've had other governesses. Other rounds. Has anyone ever made it past seven?"
He sets the cup down. The ceramic sounds louder than before.
"No."

No. The word is clean, absolute, a door closing. But the flicker — that micro-expression, that half-second of *interest* — tells me something he didn't intend to say. It tells me the answer isn't *no.* It tells me the answer is *not yet.*

I don't push. Not here. Not now. Information is a finite resource, and I've spent enough of it this morning. I have Jessel's journal. I have the rules. I have the knowledge that Round 7 — Violence — begins tomorrow.

And I have something else forming since I first read Jessel's cramped handwriting: *Peter's daughter will have something I didn't. She'll have knowledge from the start. She can choose to play backwards.*

Play backwards. Start from the end and work toward the beginning. Take the last round first, and move through the Game in reverse, each round easier than the last, the house losing its power as you approach the door instead of the pit.
It's a strategy. But it requires surviving Round 7 first.

I walk out of the breakfast room. The hallway is cold. The portraits watch me with their oil-painted eyes, the long-dead Ashfords and their long-dead judgments, and I feel their gaze the way I've always felt it — as a pressure, a weight, a house trying to know me.
But the house knows me now. And I know it.
I climb the stairs. I pass the fourth step Mrs. Grose avoids. I turn left at the landing, toward my room, and then I stop.
The control room.

Peter's files. The original design documents for the Game — if they exist, if he left behind more than the verbal inheritance Miles carries like scripture — they would be in the control room. Jessel's journal covers seven rounds. But the architecture of any game includes its end condition, its *what happens next.* Jessel never saw past Round 6. Her journal is a map that stops three provinces from the border.
There's a Round 8.

I don't know this. I can't prove this. But I *feel* it — the way you feel a room beyond a wall, the way you feel a missing step before your foot finds the gap. Seven rounds, and no one has ever survived all seven, and Miles said *no* when I asked if anyone had made it past seven, and that *no* was a door, and doors have other sides.
Round 8 exists. It was designed for the player who survives everything.
For Peter's daughter.
The control room is at the end of the east wing, behind a door I've walked past a dozen times without seeing — or without *letting* myself see. I'm not playing the old Game anymore.
I'm playing a new one.
I stand in the hallway. The fog presses against the windows at the end of the corridor. The house is quiet — Mrs. Grose upstairs, the twins at breakfast, no one watching.
I know where the files are.
And I know what I have to do: survive Round 7, find Round 8, and learn the end of a game that was written for me before I was old enough to read.
The radiator clicks. The house waits.
So do I.
